answer:
(i) Consider the case where each friend has a different number of stickers, from 0 to n-1. If Marco invites two friends with collections of 0 and n-1, they will have a complete collection. If he invites two friends with collections of 1 and n-2, they will also have a complete collection. In general, if he invites two friends with collections of i and n-i-1, they will have a complete collection. Therefore, Marco needs to reserve at least n different tables to avoid having a complete meal at the same table.

(ii) We can use a graph theory approach to solve this problem. Let's construct a graph G with n vertices, where each vertex represents a friend. Two vertices are connected by an edge if the corresponding friends have a complete collection. We want to show that G is a bipartite graph, i.e., its vertices can be divided into two disjoint sets such that every edge connects a vertex in one set to a vertex in the other set.

We can color the vertices of G with two colors, say red and blue. We start by coloring the vertex corresponding to the friend with the largest collection red. Then, we color the vertex corresponding to the friend with the second largest collection red, and so on. We continue this process until we have colored all the vertices. Since each friend has a different number of stickers, we will never have to color a vertex red that is already colored red.

Now, let's consider two vertices u and v that are connected by an edge. If u is red and v is blue, then the friend corresponding to u has a complete collection with the friend corresponding to v. If u is blue and v is red, then the friend corresponding to v has a complete collection with the friend corresponding to u. In either case, we can seat the friends corresponding to u and v at different tables.

Since G is a bipartite graph, we can divide its vertices into two disjoint sets U and V such that every edge connects a vertex in U to a vertex in V. We can seat the friends corresponding to the vertices in U at one table, and the friends corresponding to the vertices in V at another table. This way, no two friends with a complete collection will be seated at the same table.

Therefore, n tables are always sufficient for Marco to achieve his desire.

instructions:
You are a math problem solver.

Work through the problem step by step. When you have the answer, respond
with ONLY \boxed{...} as the final line of your response. Example:

The \boxed{} expression must be the last content in your response.
